# US Stock Watcher Next-Session Forecast — Friday 7/10

> **[Friday 7/10 session outlook] bullish lean — Moderate / selective risk-on · composite +30 · confidence 45%**

> **🔮 Next-session forecast** · The next U.S. regular session is Friday 7/10 (opens 9:30 AM ET / 22:30 KST 7/10).

_Data as of: 2026-07-09T22:44:09 · data quality: fresh_

## Executive summary

With the market currently closed, this brief looks ahead to the next U.S. regular session, Friday 7/10, which opens at 9:30 AM ET (22:30 KST on 7/10). Heading into that session, the overall market-state composite reads +30 out of 100, placing conditions in a "Moderate / selective risk-on" posture, with confidence at 45% based on four measured components. Several inputs remain unmeasured for this cycle — Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning have all been reweighted out of the current read — so the signal should be treated as directional rather than exhaustive.

## Market diagnosis

**Indices.** Entering Friday's session, the major benchmarks carry forward a broadly positive tone from the prior close: the S&P 500 stands at 7,543.64, up 0.81% on the day; the Nasdaq-100 sits at 29,727.10, up a stronger 1.62%; the Dow is at 52,487.41, up a more modest 0.27%; and the Russell 2000 trades at 2,992.54, up 1.22%, suggesting small-caps have been participating alongside the mega-cap-driven advance.

**Market drivers.** Four forces are shaping the setup for Friday. Volatility is supportive, as is the index trend, and the yield curve is likewise adding a supportive tailwind. The lone headwind among the tracked drivers is the US dollar, which is working against the risk-on case even as the other three factors align in its favor.

**Breadth & concentration.** The composite market-state score of +30/100 anchors the "Moderate / selective risk-on" characterization, with confidence at 45% across the four components that could be measured. As noted above, Liquidity, Credit, Earnings, Macro surprises, Valuation, and Positioning were not part of this read and have been reweighted out, so investors should treat the confidence level as reflecting a partial picture.

**Sector & rotation.** Rotation into Friday favors Financials, Health Care, and Industrials, which lead the sector tape, while Consumer Staples, Communication Services, and Energy lag — a pattern consistent with a selective risk-on tilt rather than a broad-based rally.

**Rates, dollar & commodities.** In the rates and commodities complex, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.56, while the dollar index (DXY) is at 100.94, down 0.11% on the day. In energy, WTI crude has slipped to 71.81, down 2.33%, even as gold has pushed higher to 4,132.80, up 1.52%, a combination that points to some cross-asset hedging alongside the equity strength.

## Outlook

**Short term.** Looking specifically at Friday 7/10's session (opening 9:30 AM ET / 22:30 KST 7/10), the near-term bias is constructive, consistent with the "Moderate / selective risk-on" market state and its 45% confidence reading. That said, this view is conditional: it would flip should the overall composite score cross back below zero.

**Medium term.** Over the medium term, the same constructive bias holds, again underpinned by the "Moderate / selective risk-on" designation and 45% confidence. As with the short-term view, a break of the composite score below zero would be the trigger to revisit this stance.

**Medium-to-long term.** Extending further out, the medium-to-long-term read remains constructive on the same basis — the "Moderate / selective risk-on" state at 45% confidence — with the identical caveat that a move of the overall score below the zero line would invalidate the current lean.

## Top news

Coverage feeding into this setup includes: "Nasdaq ends sharply higher; chip surge offsets Iran worries" (Reuters); "Asian Stocks Set to Gain After US Chipmakers Rally: Markets Wrap" (Bloomberg.com); "Treasury yields climb amid Iran tensions" (The Star); "US strikes on Iran send oil higher and stocks lower as rate fears grow" (USA Today); "S&P 500 closes higher as chip stocks rise and oil prices slide" (CNBC); and "S&P 500, Nasdaq, Dow End Higher Led By Chipmaker Stocks As Investors Look Past US-Iran Hostility — ORCL, SBUX, WULF, PANW, FATE In Focus" (Stocktwits). Collectively, these headlines frame a market weighing chipmaker-led strength against lingering geopolitical risk tied to Iran.
